trajectory_summary: The call opened with a legitimacy gap that was never closed, drifted into scope-creep territory as Corey attempted to explain solar economics in response to Richard's business-model questions, and ended with a hard refusal after two guilt-as-objection exits. Energy leaked steadily from the first legitimacy probe onward — Corey never anchored bill pain, never completed verify, and never created curiosity. The free-estimate pitch at the end was the right instinct but was delivered too late and too long, after Richard had already mentally exited.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the outcome is a hard no-book — Richard explicitly refused twice and the call ended without an appointment, callback time, or any forward commitment. There is no ambiguity in the outcome.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Scope-creep discipline: bridge to specialist instead of explaining solar economics
primary_coaching_focus.quote: So with with Solar, you gotta realize it's, like, by system sizes, not by because we're not charging you per kilowatt that you use.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Corey spent the middle third of this call attempting to explain solar pricing mechanics in response to customer questions he had no business answering. The explanations were incomplete, didn't land (Richard asked the same question twice), and consumed time that should have gone to verify and bill anchoring. This is the single most replicable failure pattern on the call — every 'how does this work' question Corey answered was an opportunity to use the escape hatch instead. Until this reflex is built, Corey will keep losing calls in the same place.

key_moments.quote: My name is Corey. I'm with Ion. I was just reaching out because I got a request that you were looking at solar for your home.
key_moments.stewart_read: Corey's intro uses vague 'I got a request' framing instead of the codex-required 'the solar form you filled out online.' The distinction matters: 'I got a request' sounds like a cold call; 'the form you filled out' anchors the call as customer-initiated. Richard's immediate legitimacy probe ('are you tied with Pacific Power or is what's the deal?') is a direct consequence of this framing gap.

key_moments.quote: I I don't know where you're calling from, but I live in Astoria, and it's overcast most of the time. So Yeah. So because no one has solar here.
key_moments.stewart_read: Richard raises a geographic objection — Astoria's overcast climate — that is a real, addressable concern. Corey's response (02:15) is to repeat the bill-swap description rather than engage with the objection or redirect to the specialist. The overcast concern is a legitimate qualifier question that a specialist could address with actual data; Corey neither validates it nor bridges to the specialist's expertise. This is a knowledge gap compounded by a missed curiosity-creation opportunity.

key_moments.quote: So with with Solar, you gotta realize it's, like, by system sizes, not by because we're not charging you per kilowatt that you use.
key_moments.stewart_read: Corey is now explaining solar pricing mechanics — system-size billing vs. per-kilowatt billing — in response to Richard's 'how do you make money' question. This is closer-tier content. Verify has not been completed. Bill pain has not been anchored. The rep has been pulled entirely off the setter's structural path by a customer question he should have bridged to the specialist. The explanation is also incomplete (it trails off mid-sentence), which compounds the confusion rather than resolving it.

key_moments.quote: So at this point in time, we don't pay anything. And the solar But
key_moments.stewart_read: Richard is trailing off toward a question about what happens after the system is paid off — a genuine curiosity signal buried in a confused sentence. More importantly, his framing ('we don't pay anything') is a flip opportunity per analysis_directives.flip_opportunity_detection: the 'we pay nothing' frame could be redirected to 'right now you're paying Pacific Power every month — the question is whether that payment goes to them or to a system you own.' Corey's response (04:28) pivots to scope-discipline transparency ('I'm only in the scheduling department'), which is the correct move for the question Richard was building toward — but the flip opportunity in Richard's own framing goes untouched. Note: Richard's use of 'we' here is a possible signal that a household member is involved in this decision, but the transcript contains no other evidence of a co-decision-maker. Worth the closer noting, not a confirmed dynamic.
